sentences.text: Right?
Yes.
And several days later, after I talked to Zauer, my father was brought into Kaiserwald.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Terrible condition.
He was more dead than alive.
They were working in a stone quarry.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was in terrible, terrible condition.
And I was able, through Zauer, through the commandant, after my father was in Kaiserwald, to get a job for my father in the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] an office job.
I-- it's too difficult to explain what type of work.
But an office job.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And with the extra rations I was getting, buy sandwiches from him, I was able to feed my father and almost bring him back to his normal strength and health again.
And my sister was in another camp.
And I approached him again.
I said, what are the chances, Commandant, for my sister to be here with us?
My father is here.
I'm here.
You know, I'd like to have the whole family together.
And he arranged for my sister to come.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So it was a blessing for me to have worked for him.
He was a beast.
He was an animal.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He had no regard for human life or the things he did to other people.
But I was somehow able to get a relationship going with him that he helped me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] More or less, I had more-- more food that I could eat.
And in spite of whatever animal he was, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think he helped me to survive.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But as things progressed, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] things got worse in Kaiserwald itself.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] People were shot on a daily basis.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were taking away.
People didn't return, didn't come back.
Several people tried to escape.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They caught them, brought them back, killed 10 other people who had been the same barracks with them in front of everybody.
Just shot 'em.
It was a horrible experience and yet I counted my blessings.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Emotionally, it was-- it was just almost impossible to handle.
But I knew I had to go on.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The end of-- rather, this went on-- my position or my job went on 'til 1944, 'til about August or September 1944.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father was still with me.
He was doing all right.
My sister was still with me in Kaiserwald, and she was doing OK.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In 1944, in September, Kaiserwald was liquidated.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Russian troops were marching westward onto Riga.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I suppose the Germans didn't want to leave us behind, those who were still able to work.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the end of September [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were all taken out of Kaiserwald, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] including my father and my sister, put on a ship [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] out of Riga, out of the harbor of Riga, and shipped back into German-occupied territory, into Danzig.
Danzig is the capital of-- I don't know what it is today.
Poland.
No, it's not Poland.
Gdansk, to better identify it.

And from there we-- we arrived in-- in Danzig or Gdansk, got off the ship, and were transported-- it's-- I'm a little vague and a little foggy there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how we were transported from the ship to Stutthof.
And Stutthof was a concentration camp, full-blown concentration camp, in existence approximately I'd say about 25, 30 kilometers from-- from Gdansk, from Danzig.
We arrived there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to just horrible, horrible conditions.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Instead of having barracks with 100 people, there were barracks with 500 people.
Instead of having 4, 5, or 6 people in a bunk, there were 7, 8 people in a bunk.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Food was almost nonexistent.
They used to dish out a broth, what we used to call a [NON-ENGLISH], a cabbage soup, a bowl of soup a day.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But worst of all was that there was raging [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] diseases in-- in-- in Stutthof, typhoid, typhus, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] cholera.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the conditions were-- if we thought-- if I thought the conditions were bad in ghetto and in Stutthof, Riga-- I mean, conditions were bad in ghetto and in Kaiserwald, Stutthof was hell.
No food, just a bowl of soup a day.
No work commandos.
Stay either in a barrack or you're standing outside to-- to be counted or punished, one or the other.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my father was there and my sister was in the women's camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I would see my sister occasionally on the other side of the barbed wire.
Whenever I had an opportunity, we somehow found each other, were able to wave to each other, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] couldn't talk much to each other.
But I knew she was alive and she knew I was alive.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And as conditions in Stutthof continued to get worse and worse and worse, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I found out that my sister was working again in a army clothing depot, where they were separating clothing.
It was very cold in the winter of '44-- '43-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no, winter of '44, '45 in Stutthof.
And since my sister was working in this army depot, she was able to steal some clothes and was able to get some clothes to myself and my father.
Clothes, of course, didn't do me much good anymore, because I was-- all of a sudden got sick, came down with typhoid and typhus fever.
And there was no cure for it.
There were no doctors.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The only thing that was available to us [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were some delousing pills, which the Germans issued to us hopefully not to spread the disease any further.
And what it was was a-- I can still see it in front of my eyes-- was two yellow pills we were given.
Dissolve it in water, soak your clothes in them, and then put your wet clothes on, wintertime.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The dehydration of the water through your body's warms-- warmth was supposed to kill the lice and the gnats, which are the eggs the lice were laying in-- in your clothes.
But that was just a pipe dream, you know.
Maybe it did do some good, but as a whole it didn't.
I deteriorated really, really quick.
And I was in bad-- in really bad condition, delirious, high fever.
And every day they would go through the barracks, pull people out of barracks to march them to [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] the crematorium.
Stutthof had a crematorium.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And the crematorium was inside the camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The smoke and the odor from burning flesh, people, was horrendous.
And I was taken one morning, they picked me up, put me in a group of people who were supposed to go to quote 'the shower.' But obviously we knew better than that.
We had no place to go.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was able-- had enough-- seemingly had enough [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] common sense and enough [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] resolute in myself, in my system [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that I walked away from the column of people who were on their way to the crematorium.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember being caught by an SS guard seeing me move or walk away from this column of people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember being beat up by him, telling me to get back into the column.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I don't remember much after that.
Seemingly, I didn't have enough strength to go back into the column.
Next thing I do remember is being back in the barracks.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Eventually I got better [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] came out of it again.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I didn't eat, couldn't eat.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] There was very little left of me, but the will to-- to live was there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] As time went on-- this was '44, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] end of '44.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then the early part of '45, I miraculously recuperated [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and got stronger.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And if you ask me who helped me, I don't know, but people helped me.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember an incident.
I was sleeping in a bunk with five or six people.
And obviously it was cold in the early part of-- of '45.
This must have been January or February.
And the only thing that keep us warm was all of the bodies next to each other.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I remember waking up one morning-- or one night.
This must have been during the night.
And I was freezing cold.
I was terribly, terribly cold.
And I thought I was coming down with a fever again and I was getting sick again.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I was trying to pull my jacket I had over my shoulders.
And I was laying in a really bad position.
And I was nudging the man laying next to me-- it was an older man-- to move a little bit [BACKROUND NOISE] so I could lay down comfortably.
And he did not move.
I was laying on the outside of the bunk.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And as the night went on, I felt colder and colder.
And I finally talked to this man.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I finally realized that he was dead.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was laying there ice cold.
And the cold of his body [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] generated into my body.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I'd been sleeping practically all night long with a dead man.

[PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] Well, they hauled him out the next morning, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] put him on the wagon like so many others, stiff as a board, and took him over to the crematorium, burned him.
I mean, I-- I could tell you experiences like that, similar to that, numerous ones.
But there are too many of them.
Too many of them have been-- not too many.
Many of them have been told already.
And I don't want to just be repetitious and tell them over and over again.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The early part in 1945, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] as I said earlier, I seemingly had some help from other people who were in the barracks, gotten some extra food.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father was still alive.
And he was very sick too.
And I couldn't help him, obviously, because I couldn't even help myself.
But I'd gotten stronger as I went along and my father got worse.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I hadn't seen my sister for quite a while.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Approximately March or April-- I think it was April-- in 1945, again the Germans decided to liquidate Stutthof, because the Russians were knocking at the doors.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they took us all out into a large assembly.
And they segregated those who were capable of walking [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from those who were very sick and not capable of walking anymore.
I was capable of walking.
They put me in one group.
And my father was not capable of standing on his feet anymore.
They put him in another group.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they sent the people who were not capable of walking anymore back into the barracks.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were still outside at this assembly.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And after a while, they told us that we could go back in the barracks and get whatever our personal things were we had, which was nothing, you know, and report again in 5 minutes or 10 minutes, whatever it was, at the assembly.
They had all our names, so you had to comply.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Didn't have to comply, but the consequences of not complying was death.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I said goodbye to my father.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he wanted me to stay with him.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I wanted to and I didn't want to.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was really a difficult choice for me to make, to leave him there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My dad said to me, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] stay with me, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] stay with me.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I said, Papa, I can't.
I have to go.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And I went, said goodbye to him.
Obviously, that's the last I saw of my father.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I don't know what happened to the women in-- I guess the same thing happened to the women, what happened to us.
They put us into marching groups [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] shortly thereafter, after we got back to the assembly.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we marched from [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Stutthof back to Danzig.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We stayed overnight in a holding camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was called Rehagen.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What a terrible, terrible place.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was just like [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] a terrible nightmare.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The SS came in and just [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] killed people left and right, beat them to death, shot them to death.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I-- I have a feeling that they were drunk [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and came back from their party or whatever and just went through the camp we had there.
Weren't too many people there, probably about 120, 140 people.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the next morning they hauled all the dead bodies out of the barracks.
And those who were still alive and on their feet were assembled.
I was one of them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we all marched out to the harbor.
On the march from Danzig to Rehagen, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we probably lost half of the people which started in Stutthof.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Couldn't continue marching, walking, or killed on the wayside, died on the wayside.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
And of course, the thought always went through your mind, when it's your turn.
You going to be next?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Do what you can to stay alive.
I always used to say that to myself.
Do what-- do what you can to stay alive.
Maybe you'll make it.
Make it to what?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had no idea.
I had no idea what was going on.
I had no idea about that the end of the war was imminent.
I had no idea that there would be another world for me, there would ever be a world for me.
So you're just fighting [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] against dying.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] For what purpose and for what reason?
It's really unclear to me what I personally fought for to stay alive, because all the signs and all [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the environment around me didn't lend itself to wanting to be alive.
And yet, the force within me told me, keep fighting, stay alive.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Well, that morning in Danzig, we were marched over to the pier.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were put on a boat, crew of approximately maybe 250 people, men and women.
The boat was a barge.
It was not a boat.
It was a barge, which was pulled by a tug boat.
And of course, the question was, where are we going?
Took me back to my first trip on a train when we left Gemunden.
Where are we going?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Nobody knew where we were going.
The SS was not on the barge with us, but they were on the tug boat.
We were heading out to the-- to the ocean.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Very, very desperate times.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Speculation.
They're going to sink the boat.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They'll throw you overboard.
I didn't know how to swim.
What's going to happen to you, Bert?
Don't know.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Needless to say, they didn't sink the boat, didn't throw us overboard.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we went out to the sea and we stayed on the sea, North Sea, going whatever direction we were going into-- none of us knew-- for I believe it was five or six days.
No food.
No water.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And there were some people on the barge with us, our own people, who were old enough and knew enough-- something, obviously, I didn't know, no reason to know-- don't drink saltwater, the ocean water.
It's going to kill you.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Thirst is a very, very tough thing to overcome.
Hunger, you can-- I mean, we've done without food many, many times for days and days and days.
But we had some liquid.
We had something to drink.
Well, on that barge, we had nothing to drink.
And the first day, of course, everybody, you know, held themselves back not to drink any ocean water.
And there were some people on board the second day who felt it was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a losing battle they were fighting.
And they just jumped overboard, drowned themselves.
Tried to talk 'em out of it.
[INAUDIBLE] what's the use, the sense to live?
I mean, this is no life.
And after we suffer more, where are we going to wind up?
Suffer more again?
We're sick and tired of suffering.
We want to die.
Jumped overboard.
Other people couldn't restrain themselves from drinking saltwater.
They literally drank a lot of saltwater.
I think it took a day or two.
People went berserk, went out of their mind.
Seemingly the saltwater reacted, affected their brain.
And they went berserk and they jumped overboard and went in the water.
